engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: The recapture that gave the game back
mistakes.explanation: 24.gxh5 had finally taken the knight, but you were still on top — 24…h3! shoves the passed pawn into the g2-knight's face and keeps your queenside pawns rolling. Recapturing with 24…Rxh5 parked the rook on the e2-bishop's diagonal, and 25.Rxf6! ended the game on the spot: the f6-rook is untouchable (your king on g8 doesn't reach it), while Bxh5 scoops your rook next move. In two moves an equal-ish fight became a lost one.

drillLinks.label: Capture the defender — Lichess puzzle theme

takeaways.lesson: A recapture is a move like any other — check the landing square.
takeaways.detail: Recaptures feel automatic, so they skip the blunder-check. Give the piece's new square the same one-second scan you'd give any move: who attacks it, what did it stop defending? Here h5 stood on the e2-bishop's diagonal, which turned an 'obvious' recapture into the losing move.

takeaways.lesson: When material is already gone, look for compensation, not revenge.
takeaways.detail: After 24.gxh5 the knight was lost either way. Instead of evening the ledger, ask what the position still offers — 24…h3! found it: a runner on h3 and connected queenside passers. Play the position in front of you, not the one from two moves ago.

mistakes.title: Retreated to the corner with the door hanging off its hinges
mistakes.explanation: 17.g4 lunged at your h5-knight — but it also left White's position hanging by the f3-thread. 17…exf3! wins on the spot: if 18.gxh5 then 18…fxg2 19.Bxg2 Nxe3+! forks the king on d1 and the queen on c2, and if 18.Nxf3 then 18…Bxg4 wins a pawn while pinning the knight to the king. The engine had wanted …exf3 for six moves running — the e4-pawn was your crowbar into the uncastled king — but 17…Bh8 tucked your best bishop into the corner and left the knight hanging on h5 anyway.

takeaways.lesson: Meet a flank attack with a strike in the centre.
takeaways.detail: 17.g4 was a wing lunge by a king still stuck on d1 — the textbook moment for the classical recipe. When your opponent pushes pawns at your pieces on the edge, check your central pawn breaks before you retreat anything.

takeaways.lesson: Winning positions are converted by opening lines, not by avoiding contact.
takeaways.detail: At +5, look each turn for the pawn break or capture that opens files toward the enemy king — …exf3 was available (and best) on six consecutive turns. If you catch yourself making quiet retreats while winning, that's the signal to hunt for the breakthrough instead.

mistakes.title: Fourth invitation declined — and the knight finally fell
mistakes.explanation: Your knight had been standing on h5, attacked by the g4-pawn, since move 17 — and for four moves in a row the engine's answer was the same jump: 23…Ng3!, protected by your h4-pawn and forking the f1-rook and the e2-bishop. After 24.Ba1 Nxf1 25.Bxf1 you are up an exchange with a winning game. 23…Qd7 developed calmly instead, as if the knight were safe, and White finally accepted the gift: 24.gxh5 took a whole piece for free.

takeaways.lesson: Every move, list which of your pieces stand attacked.
takeaways.detail: Before considering plans, do the one-second census: which of my pieces are attacked, and how many times? The h5-knight was en prise to a pawn for eight straight moves — a scan that costs nothing catches that on move one, not move eight.

takeaways.lesson: An attacked piece would rather move with a threat than be defended.
takeaways.detail: When a piece must move, check first the squares where it lands with a fork, a check, or an attack. …Ng3 saved the knight and won material in the same tempo — the difference between retreating under pressure and escaping with profit.

mistakes.title: The toughest defence was still on the board
mistakes.explanation: After 25.Rxf6 the damage was real but not final: 25…Rg5! slides the rook off the e2-bishop's diagonal, keeps it active on the g-file, and leaves you down a piece but still fighting — your h4- and c4-pawns give White real problems to solve. 25…Bh3 created a one-move threat and abandoned the rook: 26.Bxh5 took it, and from a full rook down there was no way back.

drillLinks.label: Loose pieces drop off — blunder-proofing discussion

takeaways.lesson: When a blow lands, save your biggest hanging piece first.
takeaways.detail: After a tactic hits you, triage before you counter-attack: which of your pieces are hanging, and which is worth most? Move or defend that one — the h5-rook outranked any one-move threat the bishop could invent.

takeaways.lesson: Make your opponent win the won game.
takeaways.detail: Down material, your job becomes resistance: active rook, running passers, no free moves for the attacker. …Rg5 followed by …c3 would have forced White to keep finding precise moves; …Bh3 collapsed in one.

highlights.note: …e5 struck at the centre before White had finished developing — the engine's top move, played by only 9% of your level. Your best chess this game came right here.
5…a5 hit the overextended b4-pawn immediately — barely 1% of your level finds this, and the engine confirms it as best. This is where you won the initiative.

openingReport.note: You met the Queen's Gambit with the Marshall Defence 2…Nf6 — playable, but theory prefers 2…e6 or 2…c6, and only White's 3.c5?? let you equalize on the spot. You have now reached the 1.d4 d5 queen's-pawn structure in three analyzed games — 20 minutes on the main replies would pay for itself.
